1. the heaven and the earth . ] the earth be indeed a muddy , sordid , and feculent place , the receptacle of all corruption : well may that perish . but the heaven be pure , bright , ceruleou , sublime , and refulgent ; Vers. 44. but raise a spiritual body : it be sow in dishonour , raise again in honour , sow in weakness , raise in power , etc now the variety of word here use of honour and power , serve special , to set forth the manner of the rise again of the faithful , all other shall also have spiritual body like unto the damn spirit in hell , that be ever continue , seek death through the greatness of the misery , but not be able to find it . that we shall have the same body , Job also testify , say : i shall see he , not with other , but with these eye . God do not only give we our being , and preserve we in it , but support and sustain we by his concurrence in all our action . for he as universal and supreme agent , have his influence into all the action and operation of inferior agent , so that in he our both live and move and have our being , and without he we neither move nor work , we can do nothing ; yet in that by his concurrence we immediate do this or that work , it be say to be from we ; but if it betrue good , the goodness of it be immediate from God grace work in we , both to will it , and to do it of his good pleasure . we must always lay this for a ground , that good work must flow from the fountain of faith , without which it be impossible to please God ; omne etenim pietatis opus , Prosp. in epigram . nisi semine recto exoritur fidei , peccatum est . every pious work be sin , if it grow not out of the right seed of faith . in the next place we be to consider , that the goodness of a moral act depend of two thing : first , the object ; second , the circumstance . Qui habitat . ante finem mihi . page . 559. to poverty , say , it be better for i , my lord , to be in tribulation , so thou be with i , than to reign , feast , or glory without thou : it be much better to embrace thou in tribulation , to have thou with i in the furnace of poverty , than to be even in heaven without thou . what be we afraid of ? why delay we ? why seek we to avoid this furnace of poverty ? the fire be terrible , but our lord be with we in tribulation : if God be with we , who can stand against we ? let we therefore , that we may take no harm by poverty , serious revolve this in our mind , that all poverty , whatsoever it be , be lay upon we by God for our great good , that we may be thorough refine from our dross . even as a tender and love mother when she behold her child play abroad in the yard , fierce assault by turkey-cocke and hen ( because he be in a red coat ) with wing raise , train display , and bristle crest ; present step to he and take he from those furious fowl , and ( though the child cry and mourn to see himself disrobe ) she pull off his scarlet wherewith turkey natural become so fierce , and all this she do for the good of her little one , not move at all with his fond and childish tear : let he weep and spare not , so he may escape this imminent danger . so God oftentimes unclothe we , take away our good , oppress we with poverty , but all for our good , for by this mean we be deliver from many danger , from the assault of the devil , and snare of manifold sin : but we like child cry and lament , wrangle and complain that we be deprive of necessary , and bring to utter beggary . oh you foolish babe ! why weep you , why complain you ? all this be do for your good . Answ. give i leave i pray to tell you , Seneca convince you of untruth , who direct say that there be no evil in poverty , so a man keep himself Sen. consol . ad hell c . 9 post medium . free from the frenzy of avarice , which subvert all . oh you that be poor ! whosoever you be , what think you of such a father who see his little son with bread in his hand , and a mastiff lie in wait , and ready to bite he by the finger ? be it not a point of wisdom in he , and a wary foresight of future mischief , rather than a deprive the child of victual , to snateh the bread out of his hand ? so God sometime deprive we of food and temporal felicity , not to impoverish or famish , but to reclaim we from sin . be not virtue for the most part banish , and all kind of vice embrace in their kingdom , where man fare dainty , sleep large , and spend the day idle . happy therefore be the poor , who willing embrace poverty , entertain it as a special friend to virtue . to this end be that generous speech of saint Paul utter : the thing which be to i a gain , the same i deem for Christ a detriment . no man be worthy of God , but he that know how to contemn riches , and he be true rich who agree well with poverty . to this purpose Diogenes say : poverty be a virtue which be learn of it self . the matter be plain : shall not riches be take from we we shall be undo and destroy by they . and who be he that can so wary touch these thorn , and not wound his own conscience ? poverty alone be not wound by this thorny brake . he abhor not poverty who aspire to bless immortality . for as true say saint Gregory : whosoever he be that fix his desire upon eternity , can neither be deter with poverty , nor shake with adversity . IV. a garland of straw . the garland of straw signify all kind of scoff , derision and contempt . this be a terrible and grievous punishment to scholar at school , how light soever it seem to other . some time it happen in school that the master command a boy to stand in a place by himself with rod in his hand . this be a more grievous punishment to that boy , than to be ten time beat with rod . in Spain they put in mockery a paper hat upon those who they lead to execution . after this manner Andronicus a emperor with considerationes g•ernita•is consid . 5. 53. of the east be crown with a wreath of garlic , set upon the back of a scab camel , and lead through the city in a miserable triumph . to be contemn , deride and scoff at , a proud man deem one of the great punishment that may be inflict . this crown of straw seem to some all lead , beset with prick like a bristle hedgehog . for we abhor nothing more than to be disgrace , brand with ignominy , or force to the blush in presence of other . This often time seem more grievous than death it self . hence it be that many guilty person kill themselves in prison rather than they will be make a object of scorn and contempt to the world . at the latter day when every one shall return from death to life , that terrible arraignment in the sight and presence of all mankind , that call to account and point out by the voice and finger of the judge , that stern eye fix on the whole world shall more torment the damn then the very flame of hell . for this cause shall man say in most desperate manner to the sleepy rock and mountain , fall you upon we , and to the hill cover we . then shall it seem to they a gentle punishment to be bury al•ve under the heavy weight of these mountain , than to be arraign at the tribunal seat of Christ , to receive that heavy sentence , and be proclaim by the elect , the utter enemy of God Saul king of the hebrew , a notorious example of a man infinite wicked , when he hear Samuel foretell the dissolution and final catastrophe of his kingdom and fortune , the deprivation of God grace , his own reprobation , and utter ruin of all ; desire only to preserve his honour : but now honour i , say he , before the elder of my people , and before Israel . so much he fear this loss ( when all thing else be in a desperate case ) that his people shall cease to honour he . behold how Saul be able like the ostrich to digest the great calamity , as hard iron , and yet be deject with a thing which may seem to be of least account : to wear this wreath of straw he esteem more grievous than death it self . what be the cause ? he be proud . there be nothing in the school of patience more fit to suppress pride , than this garland of straw . this be the most efficacious mean to pull down arrogancy ; he that be crown with this garland be force ( though much against his will ) to qualify his lofty spirit . but above all thing this be most to be admire : we desire to be repute submissive and humble , and yet hate nothing more than humility and contempt of our self : free we from ignominy . and in a manner , all thing else seem tolerable : with this , man be wont to be most deject , they especial that be proud and not full subject . we vain persuade and flatter our self that all thing ( beside this garland of straw ) be tolerable . a very fond persuasion : which Cassianus refu•eth thus : we will , say he , have chastity Cass. collat 4. c . 1• . ◊ . of body , without chastize our flesh : acquire purity of heart , without labour and watch : enjoy carnal case and quietness , and yet abound in spiritual virtue : possess the gift of patience , yet never will beprovoked by scorn and reproach ; practise the humility of Christ without the loss of worldly honour , and serve he with humane praise and estimation . in a word , we desire to keep our head from the straw garland , and to be humble without humility . in this case we must not flatter our pride ; this garland of straw be fit for that head which most abhor it , to that alone this diadem be most suitable . elegant and learned say Seneca : Sen. do provide . c . 4. post initium . how can i be assure of thy constancy against ignominy , infamy , and popular hatred , if i see thou all thy life soothe and applaud , and follow with ascertain inexpugnable and headlong troop of flatterer . this one word Repete , rehearse the same again , vex more the reader , than any other correction how rigorous soever . but this be a apparent sign of secret pride , which like nothing worse than to be blame or shame by blush : whereupon some man , singular as well for learning as contempt of themselves , ( when their ruin be to read at dinner or supper ) purposely pronounce some word amiss , that they may be force to blush at their own dissemble ignorance .
